card.title: Why Every Snowflake Is Different
card.teaser: Not a myth — and not magic. It's because each flake takes a different elevator ride through the sky.
card.coverImagePrompt: Macro photograph of a perfect six-sided snowflake resting on a dark wool fabric, delicate dendrite arms catching rim light, soft blue shadows, hyper-detailed

lessons.title: An old boast, almost true
lessons.body: 'No two snowflakes are alike' sounds like something your grandmother said. But physicists have done the math, and she was basically right. The odds of finding two truly identical large snow crystals are so small that it's safe to say it has never happened in the history of Earth's weather.

visual.imagePrompt: A single snowflake landing on a dark-gloved hand, intricate shape in sharp focus, soft falling snow blurred behind

lessons.title: Why six sides, always
lessons.body: All snowflakes share one rule: six-fold symmetry. That's not decoration — it's chemistry. Water molecules, when they freeze, naturally bond at 120-degree angles. Stack billions of those bonds and you get a hexagonal lattice. The six sides aren't a coincidence; they're the geometry of ice itself.

keyTerms.definition: The six-sided crystal structure that water molecules form when they freeze
The branching arm of a snow crystal

visual.imagePrompt: A clean scientific diagram of water molecules bonding at 120-degree angles to form a hexagonal ice lattice, translucent blue palette

lessons.title: The path writes the shape
lessons.body: A snowflake grows as it falls. At one altitude it's dry and cold, and the crystal grows flat plates. A little lower, it's humid at -15°C, and fern-like arms explode outward. Rise on an updraft into colder air and it grows columns. Each flake takes a unique zig-zag path through these zones — and the shape is literally a record of that journey.

keyTerms.definition: The chart (first mapped by Ukichiro Nakaya) showing which snow shape forms at which temperature and humidity

visual.imagePrompt: A stylized Nakaya diagram showing different snowflake shapes arranged by temperature and humidity, scientific illustration

lessons.title: The farmer who proved it
lessons.body: In 1885, a Vermont farmer named Wilson Bentley figured out how to photograph single snowflakes through a microscope. Over 46 winters he captured more than 5,000 of them. He never found two alike. Modern physicist Kenneth Libbrecht at Caltech grows designer snowflakes in a lab and confirms it: even two crystals from the same cloud, seconds apart, take different paths and end up visibly different.
lessons.funFact: A typical cubic meter of falling snow contains a trillion snow crystals — so even a single snowstorm is an astronomical diversity event.

visual.imagePrompt: A sepia-toned historical photograph of Wilson Bentley peering through a microscope-camera rig, surrounded by glass plates of snowflake photos

lessons.title: A biography in ice
lessons.body: Every snowflake that hits your jacket is a tiny autobiography. The six-fold star is its species. The intricate arms are its life story — humidity, temperature, wind, altitude — frozen in the few minutes between cloud and ground. Then it melts, and the story's gone.

quiz.question: Why do all snowflakes have six-fold symmetry?
options: Water molecules bond at 120-degree angles, creating a hexagonal lattice
quiz.explanation: The hydrogen bonds between frozen water molecules form 120-degree angles, producing a hexagonal crystal structure.

quiz.question: What mostly determines a snowflake's final shape?
options: The exact path it takes through temperature and humidity zones
quiz.explanation: Different shapes (plates, columns, fern-like dendrites) grow at different combinations of temperature and humidity as the flake falls.

quiz.question: Who was Wilson Bentley?
options: A Vermont farmer who first photographed thousands of snowflakes
quiz.explanation: Bentley pioneered snowflake photomicrography starting in 1885 and captured over 5,000 images during his lifetime.
